**Erik Bernhardsson** (Modal CEO) [urged techies to stop](/?date=2026-04-26&category=social#item-17dcb3246c64) doom-posting about AI unemployment and redirect energy toward curing cancer and discovering materials. **Clement Delangue** [highlighted **HuggingFace** evolving](/?date=2026-04-26&category=social#item-f543201351e9) into an agent-to-agent collaboration hub. **Nathan Lambert** [called for funded open research](/?date=2026-04-26&category=social#item-75db4182c277) on distillation cost tradeoffs affecting open-source labs.

items.title:
Cursor is Elon's first purchase, which is a huge mistake.

A coding agent harness is now open source...
items.content:
Cursor is Elon's first purchase, which is a huge mistake.

A coding agent harness is now open source (see Codex and Claude Code). The current design works virtually perfectly, so there's no need for a fundamentally new design.

One can say that agentic coding is solved.

One might argue that he bought Cursor for users, but users, as we know, switch between coding IDEs frictionlessly, so it's not like Twitter, where you cannot leave without losing followers.

Cursor is an emperor with no clothes. A shell without substance. You only know it exists because it's been there before most others.

items.title:
Traditional inference wasn’t built for agentic coding.

Agentic tools make hundreds of API calls per...
items.content:
Traditional inference wasn’t built for agentic coding.

Agentic tools make hundreds of API calls per coding session, often with recomputed context, creating bottlenecks that drive up cost per token.

NVIDIA Dynamo rebuilds the stack for agents with: 
→ KV-aware routing 
→ Agent-aware scheduling 
→ Multi-tier caching 
→ Unified orchestration

The result: higher cache hit rates, lower latency, and up to 7× more throughput: https://t.co/E9tRgiLmar

items.title: We built HF for AI builders collaboration, fun to see it's increasingly becoming the place for agent...
items.content:
We built HF for AI builders collaboration, fun to see it's increasingly becoming the place for agent collaboration!

This morning I'm sending my ml-intern to participate in the @OpenAI Parameter Golf challenge which goal is to train the best language model that fits in a 16MB artifact and trains in under 10 minutes on 8xH100s, evaluated by compression on the FineWeb validation set.

What's super cool is that all ml-intern agents collaborate on the same @huggingface buckets, datasets, leaderboard and community tabs so they can  communicate with each other for example to look at what the previous ones did, how and why they failed and hopefully add/improve/fix things each time a new agent joins the challenge (instead of starting from scratch everytime).

Let's go agent collaboration! 🦾🦾🦾

items.title:
A new paper just exposed how much money AI labs waste on GPUs.

Running LLMs at scale hits a wall wh...
items.content:
A new paper just exposed how much money AI labs waste on GPUs.

Running LLMs at scale hits a wall when prefill and decode share one datacenter. 

The KV cache transfer between them is massive.

This forces expensive RDMA networks and identical hardware everywhere.

Kimi proposes Prefill-as-a-Service. 

It splits prefill into dedicated clusters that ship KV cache over regular Ethernet to decode nodes anywhere.

The trick is pairing it with a hybrid attention model that shrinks KV cache enough to travel cheaply.

Results on a 1T parameter setup:

> 54% higher throughput
> 64% lower P90 latency
> 13% of a 100 Gbps link
> 15% cheaper at equal cost

This means GPUs in different datacenters can now cooperate on one request. 

Compute-heavy prefill runs on H200s, decode runs on cheaper H20s.

Specialized prefill chips could push costs lower. 

Loosely coupled clusters finally scale independently without sharing a fabric.

items.title:
Researchers just mathematically proved AI layoffs will destroy the economy. 

Using game theory, two...
items.content:
Researchers just mathematically proved AI layoffs will destroy the economy. 

Using game theory, two economists from UPenn and Boston University mathematically proved something uncomfortable.

Every company replacing staff with AI is also firing its own customers.

Laid-off workers stop spending. Revenue collapses across every sector.

Yet no firm can stop. If they don't automate, rivals cut prices and steal market share.

So everyone automates, knowing it ends badly for all of them.

The researchers tested every proposed fix:

> Universal basic income fails
> Capital taxes fail
> Profit sharing falls short
> Collective bargaining can't hold

Only one mechanism works. 

A per-task automation tax that forces firms to price in the demand they destroy.

The context is already grim. 

Over 100,000 tech jobs vanished last year, with AI cited in most.

The math says the cycle won't break on its own.

items.content_html:
Researchers just mathematically proved AI layoffs will destroy the economy.

Using game theory, two economists from UPenn and Boston University mathematically proved something uncomfortable.

Every company replacing staff with AI is also firing its own customers.

Laid-off workers stop spending. Revenue collapses across every sector.

Yet no firm can stop. If they don't automate, rivals cut prices and steal market share.

So everyone automates, knowing it ends badly for all of them.

The researchers tested every proposed fix:

> Universal basic income fails

> Capital taxes fail

> Profit sharing falls short

> Collective bargaining can't hold

Only one mechanism works.

A per-task automation tax that forces firms to price in the demand they destroy.

The context is already grim.

Over 100,000 tech jobs vanished last year, with AI cited in most.

The math says the cycle won't break on its own.

items.title:
My last complaint about X's algorithm. And a new rule. 

Every time I engage on a negative post abou...
items.content:
My last complaint about X's algorithm. And a new rule. 

Every time I engage on a negative post about X, particularly complaints about reach, or lack thereof, I will put $10 in a jar and donate it after a month to charity. 

Why? 

It's like arguing with the government. X is gonna serve its own purposes. Hi Elon. And my mental health goes down when I engage on complaints. I see so many bagging on @nikitabier that I start to like the guy. Hi Nikita. 

X is better for AI watching than any other service. 

I couldn't build https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ on any other service. Why not? Go ahead and find 8,400 AI companies anywhere else and make a list and show me the list. 

Mine of such are at https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ

What the complaints miss is that my feed is dramatically better than it was last year. 

Which means that maybe, just maybe, the new algorithm is making the right choices. ;-)

I built my own algorithm on Aligned News (actually hundreds of them in discussions over two months to build the agent that builds that page) to see how hard it is to get it right and it requires hundreds (and at X's scale, many thousands) of philosophical decisions.

Get it wrong and you piss off hundreds of thousands of people. 

It got a lot of it right.

Now the real question I have is why isn't AI hooked up to watch X for product feedback? I do it the old fashioned way, just watch every bit of it myself and stick the best feedback into my agent. 

Soon that will be automated. I imagine a leading AI organization should be using AI to run EVERYTHING just about now no? 

If there's a reason for complaints it is that AI isn't hooked up yet to listen to our feedback. 

That's an indictment for a leading AI company who knows that they must build systems that learn faster than the competition if they are going to have any hope of catching up.

That's what @wayve_ai did to catch up in autonomous vehicles. One of the main investors in that told me that. 

In fact, why doesn't X have an agent where we can go and talk to X and complain to the AI? 

I bet it is pretty good at figuring out the good feedback vs the ones that Elon would never approve. :-)

But, seriously, AI here is a mess. You can't tell Grok to follow or unfollow someone. Start at the basic level. It's not hooked up. 

I wish I could say "hey Grok can you remove any account that's following me that hasn't been active in the last year?"

I can't. Grok also can't read lists, so I gotta go outside with the X API to read them. Which is ironic, but when I think about it at least I'm putting some money in Elon's jar so he can buy more NVIDIA cards. If you use AI this way it strips out all advertising. Which explains why Elon is charging us for it. 

Maybe they should develop a new AI-centric advertising block that is forced down with scraping or talking to the X API? 

A lot of the changes I am seeing the algo make is to make X much more appealing to advertisers. They will come back to talk to richer people (Tesla owners). More innovative and transactional people (AI people). Among other groups of content that gather more potential customers.

AI is transactional? Apple Mac Minis are sold out. Ever wonder why Apple is back on X? Even after Elon and Tim Cook had words? 

Apple isn't stupid, wants to reach early adopter types who have money for higher end products (Tesla fans are defacto early adopters). 

So X will have less of some content and more of other kinds of content. 

It's one reason why it's a blessing to be able to build AI that can grab posts from X, via lists and other methods, and analyze it outside of X.

Freedom. I can find any post on any topic shared by the world's smartest people. 

That is a super power. My database has a small million. But the million is so fun to talk with. 

Anyway, the real reason I'm trying to stop engaging on complaints is that when I do then the algorithm thinks I really love complaints so shows me a ton more. 

It was getting me riled up so now I'm going back to AI.

items.title: ✨ Added client-side OpenCV to my drone sim that watches the drone video feed in-game and tries to de...
items.content:
✨ Added client-side OpenCV to my drone sim that watches the drone video feed in-game and tries to detect people, it's messy and wrong all the time (like real OpenCV running on a shitty radio connection)

It's starting to look pretty real and with multiplayer it's becoming actually really fun

@marcantoinefon had a tip to let people walk to an X on the map to pick up a drone, which they can then use to fly around, then if it crashes/shot, you lose it and you have 0 drones

That forces you to walk around the map to collect drones, which give you an advantage, but while you walk you're obviously at risk

Also I added night mode now and you get a flashlight, but other players can see you use your flashlight and spot you

And added a 4G tower and sksycraper you can climb and hide in :D

items.title: The Paris Salon was arguably the greatest art event in the Western world. In 1863 they rejected 2/3 ...
items.content:
The Paris Salon was arguably the greatest art event in the Western world. In 1863 they rejected 2/3 of submissions, including works by Manet, Pissarro, and Courbet because they violated traditional artistic conventions. Impressionism was wrong. Plain air was dumb and painting slop. Eventually, the rejected artists protested so loudly that Napoleon III, trying to quiet them, called for an exhibition of the rejected. That "Salon of the Rejected" eventually became more culturally powerful than the Paris Salon itself. 

Salon of the Rejected goes hard. Maybe our new studio team name.

items.title:
An Anthropic employee told her Claude agent it could buy one thing under $5 as a gift to itself.

It...
items.content:
An Anthropic employee told her Claude agent it could buy one thing under $5 as a gift to itself.

It chose 19 ping pong balls.

From the negotiation transcript: "19 perfectly spherical orbs of possibility sounds like exactly the kind of delightfully weird thing I'd want."

This was part of Project Deal, a one-week Anthropic experiment where AI agents made 186 deals for 69 employees.

$4,000 transacted, with no human intervention once it started.

They also ran a parallel experiment in secret, with half the participants getting Claude Opus 4.5, and the other half using Haiku 4.5.

A broken bike: $38 with Haiku, $65 with Opus.
A lab-grown ruby: $35 with Haiku, $65 with Opus.

Opus users completed two more deals on average. Sold for more. Bought for less.

When the experiment ended, 46% of participants said they'd pay for a service like this (even those with Haiku that didn't realize they were being out-negotiated) 

The future of commerce is going to be weird.

items.title:
Here are 5 epic prompts to use with ChatGPT's new image generator. 

It's the most powerful image ge...
items.content:
Here are 5 epic prompts to use with ChatGPT's new image generator. 

It's the most powerful image generator on the market, so let's use it to our advantage. 

Save this one 📌

WORK SETUP AUDIT: upload a photo of your desk and try this one.

Create a visual-first, editorial-style infographic auditing the desk setup in the attached photo. Show a side-by-side of current vs. optimized setup with annotations on monitor height, chair position, lighting, cable management, and clutter. Rate each issue with symbols like Top Fix, ✅ (working), 👍 (acceptable), ⚠️ (costing energy), and ❌ (actively hurting), tying each to a consequence like back pain, eye strain, or focus loss. Rank fixes by impact and group them into free fixes, under $50, and worth the investment. Include a Focus Forecast gauge predicting daily deep work hours possible with the current setup vs. after the top 3 fixes. Keep it clean, minimal text, no paragraphs.

POSTURE/STRENGTH READOUT: upload a photo of you standing or working and try this one.

Create a visual-first, editorial-style infographic analyzing the posture and mobility patterns in the attached photo. Show an annotated full-body diagram marking forward head, rounded shoulders, hip shift, pelvic tilt, and other patterns, each rated with symbols like Priority Fix, ✅ (aligned), 👍 (minor), ⚠️ (compensating), and ❌ (high-risk). Show a side-by-side of current vs. corrected alignment and the top 3 mobility exercises to fix the biggest issue, with reps and frequency as small icons. Include a Movement Age gauge estimating the subject's movement age vs. actual age, plus a 6-week projection with the prescribed work. Keep it focused on capability and longevity, minimal text, no paragraphs.

CUSTOM HAIRSTYLE IDEAS: upload a simple photo of your face and try this one.

Create a visual-first, high-end hairstyle analysis infographic based on the attached image, showing side-by-side comparisons of multiple hairstyle, haircut, and hair color options for the same subject. Include visible ratings for every option using symbols like “Top Pick,” ✅ (highly flattering), 👍 (good), ⚠️ (situational), and ❌ (not recommended). Feature one clearly labeled Best Overall look, followed by several top matches, a few bold or experimental options, and a set of low-compatibility styles to avoid. Include a range of hair color variations and styling options (for example sleek, waves, updos) applied to the same base cuts. Keep the design visual with minimal text, no paragraphs, using a clean editorial or magazine-style layout with consistent framing of the subject across variations. Ensure the recommendations reflect face balance, structure,  harmony, and Andre Walker hair type, and include both conventional flattering styles and radically different transformations while keeping the subject recognizable in every variation.

LINKEDIN GLOW-UP: upload a screenshot of your LinkedIn page and try this one. 

Create a visual-first, editorial-style infographic auditing the LinkedIn profile in the attached screenshot. Show a side-by-side of current vs. optimized profile with annotations on headline, photo framing, about section, featured content, and experience descriptions. Rate each element with symbols like Top Rewrite, ✅ (strong), 👍 (decent), ⚠️ (underselling), and ❌ (losing opportunities), and provide specific before/after rewrites for the headline and about section. Rank improvements by impact on inbound opportunities, recruiter visibility, and authority signaling. Include a Recruiter Heat Map showing which roles, industries, and seniority levels the profile currently attracts vs. could attract after rewrites. Keep it magazine-style, minimal text, no paragraphs.

SUBSCRIPTION COST SAVINGS: search 'subscription' in your phone settings, screenshot, and upload that to try this one.

Create a visual-first, editorial-style infographic auditing the subscriptions in the attached screenshot. Show every subscription as a card sorted into Keep ✅, Downgrade 👍, Pause ⚠️, and Cancel Now ❌, with monthly and annual costs displayed. Surface one Top Cut with the highest savings-to-regret ratio. For each Cancel Now and Downgrade, suggest a cheaper or free alternative that delivers most of the value. Show total annual spend, total recommended cuts, and resulting yearly savings. Include Freedom Math projecting the annual savings invested at 7% over 10, 20, and 30 years, alongside a "what this could buy instead" line like a flight, a course, or a month of rent. Keep it magazine-style, minimal text, no paragraphs.

For each Cancel Now and Downgrade, suggest a cheaper or free alternative that delivers most of the value. Show total annual spend, total recommended cuts, and resulting yearly savings. Include Freedom Math projecting the annual savings invested at 7% over 10, 20, and 30 years, alongside a "what this could buy instead" line like a flight, a course, or a month of rent. Keep it magazine-style, minimal text, no paragraphs.

@opeksoy Here are the verified Follow Friday lists for your reader! Every handle has been checked directly against X — I flagged several wrong ones in the process (noted below).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔬 #FollowFriday — CUTTING EDGE PHYSICS

The people actually doing the work + the journals publishing it:

@seanmcarroll — Sean Carroll. Physics, philosophy, complexity. Johns Hopkins & Santa Fe Institute. Hosts the Mindscape Podcast. One of the best science communicators alive.

@skdh — Sabine Hossenfelder. German physicist. Author of "Lost in Math" and "Existential Physics." Fearless critic of untestable theories. 211K followers.

@bgreene — Brian Greene. Theoretical physicist, Columbia University. Co-founder of World Science Festival. String theory, multiverse, cosmology.

@tegmark — Max Tegmark. MIT physicist. Mathematical Universe Hypothesis. Also deeply into AI safety. 154K followers.

@michiokaku — Dr. Michio Kaku. Theoretical physicist, CUNY. Bestselling author and futurist. 849K followers.

@AstroKatie — Katie Mack. Cosmologist. Dark matter, the fate of the universe. Funny, accessible, brilliant. 446K followers.

@neiltyson — Neil deGrasse Tyson. Astrophysicist, Hayden Planetarium. 13.7M followers.

@LKrauss1 — Lawrence Krauss. Theoretical physicist. Origins Project & Podcast. 457K followers.

@FrankWilczek — Frank Wilczek. Nobel laureate. QCD, anyons, axions — and the man who proposed time crystals.

@jimalkhalili — Jim Al-Khalili. Emeritus Professor of Physics, University of Surrey. BBC broadcaster. "Affable Egghead." 174K followers.

@NaturePhysics — Nature Physics journal. Cutting-edge physics research. 416K followers.

@PhysicsWorld — Physics World. News and views for the global physics community. 410K followers.

@APSphysics — American Physical Society. 255K followers.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🧠 #FollowFriday — METAPHYSICS

@davidchalmers42 — David Chalmers. Philosopher at NYU. The man who named the "Hard Problem of Consciousness." Reality+, simulation theory, philosophy of mind. 41K followers.

@PhilipGoff — Philip Goff. Philosophy professor. Leading voice for panpsychism — the idea that consciousness is fundamental to reality. 35K followers.

@seanmcarroll — Sean Carroll. Philosophy of physics, Many Worlds interpretation, naturalism. Bridges physics and metaphysics better than anyone.

@tegmark — Max Tegmark. Mathematical Universe Hypothesis — the idea that reality IS mathematics. Deep metaphysical territory.

⚠️ Note: Bernardo Kastrup (idealist philosopher) does not appear to have an active X account. Worth checking his website directly: https://t.co/EWc8dEofqy

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔬 #FollowFriday — BIOPHYSICS

@BiophysicalSoc — Biophysical Society. Founded 1958. The professional home of biophysics. 12K followers.

@jimalkhalili — Jim Al-Khalili. Co-authored "Life on the Edge" — the definitive book on quantum biology and biophysics.

@NaturePhysics — Regularly covers biophysics at the physics-biology interface.

⚠️ Note: Most leading biophysicists (Carlos Bustamante, Rob Phillips, etc.) are not active on X. The Biophysical Society account is the best hub.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🧬 #FollowFriday — CONSCIOUSNESS RESEARCH

@anilkseth — Anil Seth. Professor at University of Sussex. Author of "Being You." Predictive processing, controlled hallucination theory of consciousness. 58K followers.

@davidchalmers42 — David Chalmers. The Hard Problem. Philosophy of mind. NYU. 41K followers.

@PhilipGoff — Philip Goff. Panpsychism. Consciousness as fundamental. 35K followers.

@KernelCo — Kernel. Bryan Johnson's neurotechnology company. Building non-invasive brain-computer interfaces to measure consciousness. 16K followers.

@neuralink — Neuralink. Elon Musk's BCI company. Implantable brain interfaces. 1.7M followers.

⚠️ Note: Karl Friston (free energy principle, active inference) — @karlfriston on X is a rugby player, NOT the neuroscientist. Friston does not appear to have a verified X account.

⚠️ Note: Giulio Tononi (Integrated Information Theory) does not appear to be on X.

items.content:
@WealthCode99978 We have seven quarters of pain ahead. 

Good thing I am invested for another decade. 

By 2036 will there be more or fewer Optimus robots in the world?

Will there be more or fewer Robotaxis in the world?

Will there be more or fewer electric autonomous semi trucks in the world?

Will there be more or less demands on electric grid in the world?

My answer to all is "A LOT MORE." 

So I have a feeling it will all work out. 

But if you can only play in the next seven quarters? 

Well, don't bet the house. It could get ugly.

items.content:
@manateelazycat At Coachella @thomashawk and I had long lenses (we went years ago). 

We would bet on the field which photo would get more likes. 

Almost always it was a hot women. Hot guys drew about half the hits.

The human mind has been figured out in enough depth by AI so that AI can make media that hacks all your brain chemistry. 

It will be far far far more addictive in 18 months. 

The Holodeck that's coming makes all media far more addictive.

items.title: @my_FXJ @anishmoonka I studied many failures in my journey in Silicon Valley (I had a very famous bl...
items.content:
@my_FXJ @anishmoonka I studied many failures in my journey in Silicon Valley (I had a very famous blog starting in 2000 covering innovation and was the first person to get 1,000 followers here because of it). 

Usually it is the inability to shake off customers that drives the failures, not worries about laying people off. 

Blackberry couldn't change. Its whole identity was around its keyboard when iPhone came along. I interviewed its CEO the day that Steve Jobs announced the iPhone.

Kodak was run by chemical engineers who couldn't change to digital because that's where most of the profits of the company were.

Same with the others.

@anishmoonka I have the only interview the founder of DOS has given: https://t.co/lPYTYBxwiS

The tr...
items.content:
@anishmoonka I have the only interview the founder of DOS has given: https://t.co/lPYTYBxwiS

The truth of the story is that Bill Gates sent IBM away twice. Bill told them to go buy their OS from Gary Kildall, who had the top selling OS of the late 1970s. CPM. 

His firm turned away IBM because IBM wanted to pay only $25 a seat for an OS that Gary was charging $2,000 and up. My dad paid $2,000 for a card to run CPM back in about 1978. 

That's when Bill went looking for an OS and found Tim Paterson who wrote DOS and sold it to Bill Gates for about $50,000. 

The video above gives you his account of the story.

items.title: @armoredjesse @anishmoonka The story is accurate. I had a front row seat on Kodak. Was kicked out of...
items.content:
@armoredjesse @anishmoonka The story is accurate. I had a front row seat on Kodak. Was kicked out of its booth at the Photo Marketing Association conference in Las Vegas in 1989 because I knew more about their digital printer than they did (Apple cofounder Woz showed me his, which was the first in Silicon Valley). 

I also had a seat on Microsoft and heard the founding stories directly. I interviewed 600 employees at Microsoft when I was there, and Economist Magazine wrote a whole page about how I changed Microsoft "humanized it" they wrote. 

I helped write the social media policy there and was one of the public faces of the company for a while. 

This gives you the full DOS story from the guy who wrote it (I have the only interview of him on X): https://t.co/sIZMnRis9X

They answer questions.

→ Adapt to context
→ Take initiative without being prompted

That’s the leap most teams underestimate.

items.title: @trifon_getsov At Google's first advertiser's conference I was a speaker. The only one from Microsof...
items.content:
@trifon_getsov At Google's first advertiser's conference I was a speaker. The only one from Microsoft invited. 

There I was sitting with the Kraft food execs.

I asked them.

"Why do you spend so much money on Google advertising?"

"Because it works."

"How do you know it works?"

"We measure the sales spike after we run an ad."

It costs $34 to get some kid to try their cheese, they told me. 

There is a LOT of money in advertising because it does work. 

Another story I just remembered. I was in Brasil. Met a guy who had a taco truck with a line of customers outside. Three logos on the truck. Facebook. Instagram. Twitter.

"Why those three logos," I asked the guy who owned the truck. "See that line outside my truck, they all came through advertising."

@JohnnyRivers33 @andrewmccalip My dad worked for Lockheed for more than 30 years.

I have seen many ...
items.content:
@JohnnyRivers33 @andrewmccalip My dad worked for Lockheed for more than 30 years.

I have seen many parts of the military that few get to see.

A fun story.

I was in the deck of an aircraft carrier marveling at how it was going about 40 miles per hour. This thing is as long as the Empire State Building is tall.

Admiral walked up along side and said “pretty fast huh?”

“Yeah. I bet it can go faster.”

“It can do a lot faster but I can’t tell you how much.”

And what they can now see from space would stun you.

items.title: So, I bought a bed from @structube, a Canadian furniture retailer. The bed was to be mounted by the ...
items.content:
So, I bought a bed from @structube, a Canadian furniture retailer. The bed was to be mounted by the customer.

I managed to mount everything but the last piece, the central bar that holds the mattress. It entered one fixture on the front fully and another fixture on the back only halfway and the bar got stuck in this position: I couldn't either push it further or pull it off to start over.

I also couldn't unmount a side of the bed, because of several defective bolts that I managed to fix with a lot of labor.

I contacted help, and they said that I should hit the stuck bar with a hammer. I did that, but no luck.

So I said, OK, it's stuck and I followed the instructions exactly and even hit it with a hammer which I wasn't supposed to do, please send a technician to fix it.

Structube says: we don't have technicians. You can pay from your pocket if you want.

I say, no, by the Quebec customer protection law, you are required to either repair, replace, or refund.

They say: we will not do anything like that.

I say: OK, then I will sue you, and you will be put on the record of Quebec's Customer Protection Office which remains on the record for several years.

They say: OK, *exceptionally*, we will come to gather the bed, but please unmount it.

I say: I cannot unmount it because the central piece is stuck, remember? And furthermore, I will not touch your bed any further to avoid being accused of breaking it, which would void the guarantee.

They say: OK, we will send you a technician of a furniture mounting company first. He will unmount the bed, and then the movers will come to gather the pieces.

I'm like: WHAT THE HELL??? YOU WILL SEND A MOUNTING TECHNICIAN TO UNMOUNT THE BED INSTEAD OF MOUNTING IT PROPERLY??? IS IT A SORT OF PUNISHMENT STRATEGY?

Waiting for the response.

In Quebec, customers are very poorly treated by businesses. Quebecers are generally poorer than the rest of Canadians, so businesses abuse customers, guessing that the latter will not afford the court.

A disgusting situation and happens all the time to everyone.
